title: Tesla driver who blamed crash on autopilot pressed accelerator 100%, NTSB finds
gist: NTSB preliminary findings confirmed Tesla's claim that a DoorDash driver, not Full Self-Driving, caused a fatal Texas crash by manually overriding FSD and pressing the accelerator to 100 percent after allegedly passing out, vindicating Musk's and Tesla executive Ashok Elluswamy's public rebuttal, though the family's lawsuit alleging a possible 'Sudden Unintended Acceleration' defect and NHTSA's special investigation remain open.

themes.theme: NTSB findings validate Tesla's FSD defense

insights.claim: NTSB's preliminary report confirmed that Tesla's Full Self-Driving (Supervised) was engaged at the time of a fatal Texas crash but that the driver, Michael Butler, manually overrode FSD by pressing the accelerator pedal to 100 percent before the car struck a home and killed 76-year-old resident Martha Avila.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: An independent federal investigator corroborating Tesla's telemetry-based defense reduces near-term product-liability and reputational risk for Tesla's FSD system in this specific case, a useful data point for Tesla's broader FSD safety/liability narrative even as the case remains open pending final NTSB cause determination.
insights.quote: the driver manually overrode FSD (Supervised) by pressing the accelerator pedal to 100 percent

insights.claim: Tesla VP of AI software Ashok Elluswamy publicly stated, ahead of the NTSB findings, that internal data showed the driver manually overrode self-driving by pressing the accelerator to 100 percent in the residential area — a claim the NTSB's preliminary report subsequently corroborated.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Tesla's practice of rapidly publicizing internal vehicle telemetry to rebut driver claims after high-profile crashes was validated by an independent regulator in this instance, reinforcing that pattern as a recurring PR/legal defense strategy investors should expect Tesla to repeat in future incidents.
insights.quote: the driver manually overrode self-driving by pressing the accelerator all the way to 100 percent of the accel pedal in this residential area
insights.locator: Tesla's vice president of AI software, Ashok Elluswamy

insights.claim: Phone data showed driver Michael Butler was working as a DoorDash driver at the time of the crash and had searched Google for terms like 'Tesla FSD not aggressive enough 2026' and 'Tesla FSD too timid' before the crash, suggesting he wanted more aggressive autonomous driving behavior rather than experiencing a system malfunction.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Illustrates a human-factors risk specific to partial-autonomy systems: drivers actively seeking to override conservative safety behavior, a pattern regulators (NHTSA's special investigation is open) and insurers may scrutinize when assessing FSD's real-world risk profile beyond the software's own decision-making.
insights.locator: According to a local ABC News report

insights.claim: The Avila family's lawsuit against Butler and Tesla alleges a possible 'Sudden Unintended Acceleration' defect in which increased power draw could cause the inverter to incorrectly interpret sensor input as the accelerator being pressed, rapidly advancing the vehicle to dangerous speeds, an allegation not resolved by the NTSB's preliminary report.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: A live product-defect legal theory against Tesla remains unresolved even after the NTSB corroborated the accelerator-override telemetry, meaning legal and regulatory risk (NHTSA's special investigation) is not fully closed despite the favorable preliminary finding for Tesla.
insights.locator: Although cops initially found no evidence of a mechanical malfunction
